title: TPU v1 · CDC and Reset Release Across Core, PCIe, and DDR3 Clock Domains
oneLine: A TPU-class chip spans at least three independent clock domains: the ~700 MHz compute core, PCIe host interface, and DDR3 memory interface. Single-bit controls use two-flop synchronizers, multibit data uses asynchronous FIFOs, and reset is released in an ordered sequence per domain—the practical engineering scope of clock-domain crossing and reset design.

legend.name: Core array domain (~700 MHz)
legend.desc: The 256×256 systolic array, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, and accumulator run at the main compute frequency. This domain contains the chip’s arithmetic capacity and receives traffic from the interface domains.

legend.name: PCIe host-interface domain
legend.desc: PCIe 3.0 exchanges CISC commands and data with the host using a clock recovered from the link, whose frequency and phase are unrelated to the core domain.

legend.name: DDR3 memory domain
legend.desc: The interface clock for 8 GiB of off-chip DDR3 at ~30 GB/s controls burst reads of weights into the chip and forms another independent asynchronous domain.

legend.name: Asynchronous FIFO (multibit CDC)
legend.desc: Commands, activations, weights, and other multibit buses cross through dual-port asynchronous FIFOs. Gray-coded read and write pointers are synchronized so only one bit changes per step and no torn intermediate pointer is sampled.

legend.name: Two-flop synchronizer (single-bit CDC)
legend.desc: Single-bit levels or pulses such as done, interrupt, and enable pass through two destination-domain flops to contain metastability. A 1~2-cycle latency trades for an extremely low failure probability.

steps.cap: ① Commands and activations arriving in the PCIe domain are written to an asynchronous FIFO and safely read by the ~700 MHz core domain. Independent clocks are decoupled while the FIFO absorbs rate differences and bursts.
② Weight bursts from the DDR3 domain likewise cross through an asynchronous FIFO into the core domain before weight-stationary loading of the systolic array.
③ Single-bit done, interrupt, and enable signals do not need a FIFO; a two-flop synchronizer in the destination domain contains metastability before the FSM samples them.

④ Each clock domain has its own reset. Use asynchronous assertion and synchronous release, sequenced by dependency—stabilize memory and interface domains first, then release the core—to avoid partial-reset operation.

before: Enumerate the clock domains: A TPU-class device decouples interface timing into at least the ~700 MHz compute-core domain, PCIe host-interface domain, and off-chip DDR3 memory domain. Each has an independent source, frequency, and phase. The exact partition is common multi-interface datacenter-ASIC practice, not itemized v1 disclosure.

Inventory and classify every crossing: Record every signal that crosses a boundary and separate single-bit control/status, such as done, interrupt, and handshake levels, from multibit data/address buses such as commands, activations, and weights. Their safe crossing mechanisms are different; mixing them is a primary CDC bug source.

Define frequency and throughput constraints: With a ~700 MHz core, PCIe 3.0 link, and DDR3 at ~30 GB/s, size each cross-domain FIFO from source bursts, destination consumption rate, and synchronization latency. It must not be too shallow to retain data or needlessly deep to waste SRAM.

Define reset sources and scope: Determine which resets originate from power-on, host control, or watchdog; which domains each affects; and which trained DDR3 or configured link state must survive a soft reset.
during: Cross single-bit signals safely: Use two-stage, or when required three-stage, destination-domain synchronizers to contain metastability. The source must hold a level long enough for at least two destination clocks; a narrow pulse crossing into a slower domain must first be stretched or converted to a level-and-acknowledge handshake.

Cross multibit data safely: Never synchronize each bus bit independently because bits may settle on different cycles and create a torn word. Use an asynchronous FIFO with Gray-coded pointers for streams; for sparse configuration registers, hold data stable and synchronize a 1-bit enable through a handshake.

Trade FIFO depth against backpressure: Greater depth absorbs larger bursts but consumes SRAM and adds latency. High-throughput weight and activation streams need enough depth to keep the array busy plus almost-full backpressure so the source can stop in time.

Use asynchronous assertion and synchronous release: Reset may assert immediately without a clock, but release must align to each local clock through a reset synchronizer. Otherwise flops leave reset at different times and an FSM can enter an illegal state.

Sequence reset release by dependency: Stabilize clocks and PLLs, then bring up interface and memory domains, and release the core only after its FIFOs and dependencies are ready. Reversing the order risks reading uninitialized state.

Evidence boundary: Public TPU v1 material does not disclose synchronizer depth, FIFO sizes, or reset-tree parameters. The mechanisms described are standard CDC/reset practice for datacenter-inference ASICs; the disclosed background is the presence of ~700 MHz compute, PCIe, and DDR3 interfaces.

result.label: Multibit CDC
result.value: Asynchronous FIFO with Gray pointers / handshake (common practice)

result.label: FIFO depth
result.value: Sized from burst and consumption rate, with almost-full backpressure (not public for v1)

result.label: Release order
result.value: Interface/memory ready first; core array last (common practice)

after: Run static CDC analysis: Scan all domain crossings to ensure each single-bit path has a synchronizer, every multibit path uses a FIFO or handshake, and no bus is independently synchronized bit by bit. Confirm synchronizer flops are preserved through synthesis.
Evaluate metastability and MTBF: Estimate mean time between failure for critical synchronizers at target frequencies. Add a third stage if two do not provide sufficient margin.

Simulate CDC and reset extremes: Use multiple clocks with randomized phase and jitter to cover FIFO full/empty backpressure, narrow pulses into slow domains, and reset release at adverse phases. Verify no loss, duplication, or illegal state.

Verify every reset sequence: Exercise power-on, host soft reset, and watchdog reset. Ensure the core always releases last and soft reset does not erase trained DDR3 or configured link state that should be retained.

Hand constraints to physical design: Encode asynchronous clock groups and FIFO false-path or maximum-delay constraints in SDC so STA neither reports meaningless asynchronous timing nor misses real synchronizer constraints.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the paper discloses multiple interfaces and ~700 MHz/PCIe/DDR3 facts; synchronizer depth, FIFO size, and reset-tree details are not public for v1 and follow standard datacenter-inference ASIC practice)
